title: Creating A Moore's Law For AI Scaling
gist: AI scaling is hitting fundamental power and efficiency walls, requiring 10X compute efficiency gains over a decade via full-stack co-optimization across algorithms, advanced packaging, novel device architectures, and ecosystem-wide collaboration. Emerging agentic and physical AI systems will demand 150X more compute than current LLMs, pushing semiconductors from 2D transistor scaling toward volumetric 3D integration, CFETs, and CMOS 2.0. Hardware differentiation — not just data center capacity — will define competitive advantage in the Intelligence Era.

insights.claim: AI data centers are now measured in gigawatts, and power consumption increases almost linearly with compute scale rather than improving in efficiency.
insights.speaker: Patrick Vandenameele
insights.so_what: Energy infrastructure costs are becoming a binding constraint on AI scaling, creating demand for power-efficient semiconductor solutions and raising risk for hyperscalers with linear opex growth.
insights.quote: AI data centers are now literally measured in gigawatts, and the playbook has been consistent... power consumption increases almost linearly. It does not really scale.

insights.claim: Multi-agentic AI systems are estimated to require 150X the compute power of existing large language models, making the scaling challenge far more severe than current infrastructure supports.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: This 150X figure implies enormous capex demand for next-generation AI infrastructure and creates urgency for efficiency-first chip architectures.

insights.claim: The fundamental question for the AI era is whether AI can scale without exceeding infrastructure and planetary limits, especially as physical AI enters the picture.
insights.speaker: Patrick Vandenameele
insights.so_what: Sustainability and energy constraints could become regulatory or resource bottlenecks for hyperscalers; efficiency-focused semiconductor suppliers gain strategic relevance.
insights.quote: Can we scale AI without exceeding infrastructure and planetary limits? This becomes even more urgent as we enter the era of physical AI, robots, autonomous systems, and smart environments.

themes.theme: Full-Stack Co-Optimization as the New Moore's Law

insights.claim: A 10X compute efficiency gain over 10 years is achievable because contributions from algorithms, device scaling, communication fabric, and architectural advances are multiplicative when co-optimized across the stack.
insights.speaker: Patrick Vandenameele
insights.so_what: The multiplier effect from cross-stack co-optimization is the new investment thesis for semiconductor R&D; companies able to orchestrate ecosystem collaboration (like imec) hold structural advantages.
insights.quote: The main contributors, algorithms, device scaling, communication fabric, and architectural advances are multiplicative. When we co-optimize them, the product exceeds the performance needs of AI.

insights.claim: Samsung is co-optimizing across DRAM, NAND, logic, and advanced packaging, and has developed IGZO vertical channel transistors for DRAM that reduce off-current by several orders of magnitude.
insights.speaker: Jaihyuk Song
insights.so_what: Samsung's IGZO DRAM could be a competitive differentiator in the memory market, especially for energy-sensitive AI inference workloads.
insights.quote: Switching to an oxide semiconductor channel can reduce the off-current by several orders of magnitude.

insights.claim: Samsung is collaborating with Nvidia on physics-informed AI models for plasma etching, thermal modeling, and mechanical modeling, enabling higher yield and equipment predictability.
insights.speaker: Jaihyuk Song
insights.so_what: AI-driven process control is becoming a manufacturing moat; Nvidia's reach is extending into fab operations, deepening its ecosystem lock-in.

insights.claim: System Technology Co-Optimization (STCO) — including wafer-to-wafer hybrid bonding, backside power distribution networks, and TSV interconnects — allows the industry to offset slowing transistor scaling with 3D integration gains.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: STCO is the dominant near-term lever for compute density improvement; advanced packaging suppliers and EDA vendors with 3D design tools are well-positioned.

insights.claim: TSMC reports that mobile application processors are migrating to 2nm by end of 2026, RF devices to 6nm FinFET, and image signal processors to 12nm FinFET, all driven by AI requirements at the edge.
insights.speaker: Kevin Zhang
insights.so_what: Edge AI is pulling leading-edge node demand beyond data centers into consumer and automotive applications, expanding TSMC's addressable market and sustaining leading-edge utilization.
insights.quote: We see that because of AI, the technology at the edge is being accelerated.

insights.claim: TSMC is driving operating voltage down to 0.4V to achieve up to 70% switching power reduction in edge devices.
insights.speaker: Kevin Zhang
insights.so_what: Voltage scaling at the device level is a key enabler for battery-constrained edge AI; 70% power reduction is a major competitive spec for mobile and IoT SoC vendors.
insights.quote: We are driving operating voltage down to 0.4V in order to achieve up to 70% switching power reduction.

insights.claim: Physical AI systems — robots, autonomous vehicles, hospital equipment — require sub-millisecond deterministic response times with no tolerance for retry, fundamentally different from cloud AI's latency-tolerant model.
insights.speaker: Salil Raje
insights.so_what: Physical AI creates a distinct hardware market where FPGAs, adaptive SoCs, and real-time architectures (not just GPUs) are essential; AMD's adaptive compute group is well positioned.
insights.quote: Data center training and inference are automated, latency-tolerant, and centralized, and the cloud is very forgiving... For hospitals and homes, there are no second tries.

insights.claim: The top 150 robotics OEMs require determinism, predictable timing, open architecture, and a scalable platform that works across industrial arms, AMRs, and humanoid robots.
insights.speaker: Salil Raje
insights.so_what: A unified, open platform for robotics is a high-value market gap; silicon vendors offering programmable, standards-compliant solutions for heterogeneous robot fleets have strong pricing power.
insights.quote: The top 150 robotics OEMs and developers have three asks — determinism, predictable timing, open architecture, and scalable platform.

insights.claim: Physical AI's challenges are distributed and cannot be solved by any single company; solutions must be distributed across companies, disciplines, and standards bodies.
insights.speaker: Salil Raje
insights.so_what: Standards bodies and ecosystem consortia will play a larger role in physical AI infrastructure than in cloud AI; early participants gain specification influence.
insights.quote: These physical AI systems will work in real-time, and continue to work even though parts of them could fail. The problems are distributed, and the solutions need to be distributed, too.

themes.theme: Advanced Device Roadmap: CFETs, CMOS 2.0, and Volumetric 3D

insights.claim: Imec's roadmap projects first production use of complementary field-effect transistors (CFETs) with vertically stacked nFETs and pFETs at the 7 Angstrom node around 2033.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: CFETs represent the next transistor architecture after gate-all-around; EDA vendors and materials companies aligning to 7A design rules now will have a multi-year lead.

insights.claim: CMOS 2.0 will combine a functional wafer backside with tier-based frontside layers dedicated to memory, logic, and RF, enabling system-level integration within a single chip.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: CMOS 2.0 redefines chip architecture economics; companies that develop IP and design tools for this paradigm early will be indispensable to foundries and fabless customers.

insights.claim: Imec's Eric Beyne proposes a 'volumetric 3D' package that bends the interposer around HBM modules in slots, shrinking the substrate, improving yield, and enabling microchannel cooling — currently in early development.
insights.speaker: Eric Beyne
insights.so_what: If volumetric 3D matures, it could displace today's large passive interposers (e.g., CoWoS), shifting value toward active interposer suppliers and advanced packaging specialists.
insights.quote: Suppose you are actually able to bend the interposer, and you put the HBM below the interposer in slots like a DIM card... That interposer immediately becomes smaller, and you can add microchannel cooling to the vertical slots.

insights.claim: Imec has demonstrated 16nm pitch lines and spaces (8nm lines) and 8.7nm tip-to-tip spacing using its newly installed high-NA (0.55) ASML EUV scanner.
insights.speaker: Laura Peters
insights.so_what: High-NA EUV is transitioning from lab to pilot line; this validates ASML's roadmap and signals that 2A-node patterning capability is on schedule.

insights.claim: UMC is leveraging imec's iSiPP300 silicon photonics process to develop a 12-inch silicon photonics platform, with its PDK expected to be available to general customers in 2027.
insights.speaker: Steven Hsu
insights.so_what: UMC's 2027 silicon photonics PDK availability signals a new competitive front in optical interconnects for AI data centers; alternative to InP-based solutions.

insights.claim: AI infrastructure demand for higher-bandwidth optical interconnects is driving the industry toward 12-inch silicon photonics manufacturing for improved scalability and cost efficiency.
insights.speaker: Steven Hsu
insights.so_what: Silicon photonics at scale is becoming a cost and bandwidth imperative for AI data center interconnects; wafer size migration mirrors the economics of mainstream CMOS.

insights.claim: The complexity of AI systems is pushing the semiconductor ecosystem toward earlier and deeper collaboration across design, process development, manufacturing, and advanced packaging, with tighter supply chain alignment.
insights.speaker: Germain Fenger
insights.so_what: Shared innovation models benefit EDA, materials, and IP vendors embedded in imec-style consortia; standalone companies without deep ecosystem ties face growing disadvantage.
insights.quote: The complexity of AI systems is pushing the ecosystem toward earlier and deeper collaboration, with companies working together from technology exploration through production.
